title_guess: Northern tour: Leeds, York, Hadrian's Wall, Settle, Burnley, Warburton & Arley Hall

pages.text:
John's birthday. Saturday Aug 19th
  Off to the north. Leeds - camped on the Sykes front drive. Swimming with NCT + watched canoeists afterwards. Catherine is just able to stand. We all had chocolate birthday cake.
Sun After St. Aidan's church we drove to York.
  We arrived at the Minster just after the start of evensong. Crowds carefully shut away from Christian worship. Officious officials. Scaffolding for thunderbolt repairs. Disappointing
  We rode in a horse + cart around York. &. Stone column upside down. The Mayor's House, where we later sat on the steps.
Mon We spent the night in a good campsite at __Bishopsthorpe__. There's a Marina. We [?much] took a Riverboat into and out of York. A lovely, lovely bishop's house/palace on the river. We walked around the Shambles. (Butcher's Fleshammels) The __Castle Museum__'s marvellous streets (enclosed) reconstructed streets filled with Dr. Kirk's "Bygones". (Thomas) (Merrygoround with

pages.sketch_desc: Two small sketches appear on the right page: one near the top showing what appears to be a domed/rounded structure (possibly a tent or a fairground ride), and one near the middle-right showing a simplified drawing of Clifford's Tower on its mound.
pages.text:
horse shoofte
Gilligan's
Merrygoround.
Knights and boy + girl in Edwardian dress.
(William) big block, horse + carriage, 1989 Kitchen
(Peter) - in the Shambles - playing lute, fire engine
                                    stuffed dog
(Simon) lights from past + present, Ewbank washing machine, development of kitchens.
(Eve) 2 seats side-by-side of farm loo, carved in wooden bench. Anglo-Saxon mask, Jacobean livingroom - ornately carved cradle, carpenter's frame marks, no cushions or carpets.
  Cliford's tower on man-built mound.

  We walked around the Shambles, found a loo
Sir Peter + the children watched a video on the history of York, while I fed Charles + Simon [looked] around the display.
  We came back on the boat the day after the Thames Riverboat accident. There were not enough life jackets or emergency exits on the boat

On both Sunday and Monday we managed to be ready to leave by 10. We get up at about 7.30. Lay the table, eat breakfast, wash up [illegible] breast feed Charles, solid feed him, change his nappy, change John's nappy. Put away sleeping bags, tables & chairs & tent.

Tues
    We left camp (after dogshit problems by fresh-water pipe) & found delicious Blackberries in the hedges at our coffee break, which we picked for lunch. We're on our way to __Hadrian's Wall__. Campsite lovely open field, but very windy Stonehaugh * * * * *
Chesters
William - "Daddy, why aren't people harmed by radio waves?"

Wed
    Shopping in __Hexham__, Simon, Thomas & William went to Vindolanda; Peter, John, Charles & Eve rested. Very windy night. Thomas & Peter evacuated tent at 4.am.
Thu Very wet walk to Housesteads in the after-noon. Simon bought Roman book presents for

the boys, a sewing-kit for Eve & Family Membership of National Heritage.
Thomas:- "Daddy, will I catch Huntingdon's Chorea just from looking at a picture of it? 'No, but looking at a picture of Jesus might make me more holy'"
Peter used his telescope from a lookout. Tried to dry out clothes on fierce van heater going down the motorway. We arrived at Wood End campsite, * * * * * near Austwick, near __Settle__, Yorkshire, quite sodden & all slept in the van. Charles, aged 4 months, is sleeping on a folded towel on the kitchen floor, & being bathed in the sink.
Friday One of the best days of the holiday. We went on a 3 mile walk up the Currick Valley, near Ribblesdale. Up lanes, only bridleways walled in by high drystone walls on both sides. Slow walking, lots of blackberries, streams & bridges, my favourite sort of walk. Simon carried Charles on his front & John on his back. John walked, reluctantly, then Simon

pages.sketch_desc: Two hand-drawn sketches of dry-stone walls: one showing Northumberland style (double row of protruding stones) and one showing North Yorkshire style (built-in stiles, under-holes for sheep, tall ladders for hikers).
pages.text:
went ahead & picked a few berries, respacing them on thorns lower down the bush, for John to pick his own, whereupon John's slow plod changed into a jog.
	Postman Pat country - we even saw a van. Many bridleways for walkers.
	Walls in Northumberland
often have a double row of sticking-out stones:-

[sketch of Northumberland wall with double row of protruding stones]

Whereas the ones here in North Yorkshire have built-in stiles, and under-holes for sheep, with tall-ladders for hikers.
[inserted above: longstones right through]

[sketch of North Yorkshire wall with stile, under-hole, and tall ladder]

Straight after lunch we went to Mr. Hird's riding pony trekking, for the boys, but he didn't have any space. He's a cousin of the campsite

farmer. So we went shopping in Settle instead, looking for places to sell "Decorum"
	We found an interesting shop - Holland & Daughter. (but she's married now) The owner sells hand knitwear from [strikethrough] Aran a rare breed of sheep. She might sell our Arans. She rowed with a woman on the telephone who had sent her a "hard-sell" brochure - "We're not like that in the Lakes."
	We bought delicious cakes & pies at the bakery.
	The farm shop on the campsite sells lovely fresh milk, Wensleydale cheese, eggs & cream & orange juice. Loo clean.
	The farmer & his wife were very nice. He was very friendly & patient explaining about the milking. We saw the cows being brought down for milking & watched the milking in the milking parlour - 6 at a time.
	Thomas & William sent out to sleep in the tent. Much fighting among Thomas, William & Peter.
Sat We are stopping in at Burnley to see

Mr. Rourke's factory on the way home. Much rain. __Burnley__
Wrought iron - forges & heavy hammer pounding red hot iron into a shape.
Another pushing machine puts a bend in the metal.
A third machine puts a scroll turn, spinning round, gripping the end.
To make gates & bedheads & ornate window bars.

After Burnley, we drove to __Warburton__ in Cheshire. St. Werburgh's church is falling into disrepair; it is a Redundant Church — black & white timber frame 1645, stone and 1711 brick, with a modern brick vestry, ugly; from 1927. [illegible] there at

the turn of the century.
	The cemetery has tombstones of Warburtons, William 1600 or 1700, and this century George & John Warburtons. One Warburton tombstone marked 4 dead children, too.
	Afterwards we went to __Arley Hall__, which the Warburtons moved to in about 1400.
	It is now lived in by Lord & Lady Ashbrook. In 17[illegible]. the Egertons married the Warburtons. In about 1937 the Ashbrooks married the last Miss Egerton-Warburton.
	We met the family, & I asked them if they would like a photo of our picture of Arley Hall. They seemed very nice, (so did the staff) & he had corresponded with Piers, via Sotherbys, to
